As a native-born Italian woman who works as a plumber in Milan, I deal with practical problems every day, so when I see fake posts online, it feels unprofessional and sometimes even dangerous. For example, once there was this viral post claiming you could fix your sink without calling a professional – well, that's not always true! But since my mental health is good, I try to stay level-headed when encountering such content.\n\nI believe the best way to manage fake stuff on social media is simple: don't engage too much. If I see something suspicious, like an ad promising plumbing services for half price, or a news story with no source listed, I just ignore it and move on. response_parsed.topic: How to manage and dealing with fake content on social media.
response_parsed.opinion:
I've always been cautious about the internet, especially after seeing how misinformation can spread like wildfire. As a native-born Italian woman who works as a plumber in Milan, I deal with practical problems every day, so when I see fake posts online, it feels unprofessional and sometimes even dangerous. For example, once there was this viral post claiming you could fix your sink without calling a professional – well, that's not always true! But since my mental health is good, I try to stay level-headed when encountering such content.

I believe the best way to manage fake stuff on social media is simple: don't engage too much. If I see something suspicious, like an ad promising plumbing services for half price, or a news story with no source listed, I just ignore it and move on. Being agreeable doesn't mean swallowing everything whole; I can politely disagree online without losing my temper.

My religious background as a practicing Jew also makes me wary of harmful content – anything that promotes hate or misunderstanding is automatically suspect in my view. And because I have good mental health, even when fake posts stress me out (like those misleading product reviews), I don't let it ruin my day.
response_parsed.reasoning_summary:
As someone with high neuroticism but good mental health, I tend to be cautious and skeptical about information that is easily accessible through social media. My background as a plumber requires practical judgment, making me more inclined to verify claims before acting on them. The fact that I'm native-born Italian and part of the Jewish community adds an ethical layer where authenticity and truthfulness are important values for maintaining trust in society.

Given my moderate openness score (even though it's low), I don't rush into believing new things; instead, I rely on established knowledge from reliable sources. My moderate extraversion level means that while I do interact online occasionally, especially through football-related groups or reading clubs, I'm not overly dependent on social media for validation.

Since fake content often mimics legitimate information (like misleading product reviews), my method focuses on skepticism and verification rather than constant monitoring. This approach aligns with my practical problem-solving skills from my plumbing profession.
response_parsed.tone: Skeptical but good-humored, cautious without being alarmist
